coredata.dc:title: Development of in-situ electrochemical heavy metal ion sensor using integrated 1D/0D/1D hybrid by MWCNT and CQDs supported MnO2 nanomaterial
coredata.dc:creator: Bhuvaneswari, K.
Radha, S.
Sreeja, B.S.
Senthil Kumar, P.
coredata.dc:description: An integrated 1D/0D/1D hybrid nanomaterial was prepared from MWCNT supported carbon quantum dots @ MnO2 nanomaterial for a sensitive and selective electrochemical heavy metal ion sensor by hydrothermal methods. The developed nanomaterials were characterized by various analytical methods such as FESEM, HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, EDX and elemental mapping study, and also its electrochemical properties of the prepared samples were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) analysis has been used to investigate the quantitative detection of heavy metal ions such as cadmium and chromium on modified electrodes under optimal conditions. The in-situ electrochemical sensitivity and selectivity of the samples were determined by varying various parameters, such as the concentration of heavy metal ions, different electrolytes and electrolyte pH. The observed DPV results show that prepared MWCNT (0.05 wt%) and CQD (0.1 wt%) supported MnO2 nanoparticles show effective detection response for chromium (IV) metal ion. In particular, 0D CQD, 1D MWCNT, and MnO2 hybrid nanostructures produced a synergistic effect among them, resulting in strong electrochemical performance of the prepared samples against the target metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MWCNT
Carbon quantum dot
MnO2
Electrochemical sensor
Chromium
Cadmium
Dimensional hybrid

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid misctext orcid primabst ref

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: In this work, 1D/0D/1D hybrid nanomaterials were prepared by using the MWCNT and CQD supported MnO2 to sense the heavy metal ion through electrochemical analysis. The 1D/0D/1D hybrid nanomaterial containing CQD distributed on 1D MWCNT and MnO2 hybrid nanoparticle was formed by in-situ hydrothermal process. The structural, morphological and elemental analysis of the prepared samples were investigated through various analytical methods. In addition, the electrochemical properties of the as-prepared samples were examined using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Most of the recent research on electrochemical sensors has focused on the detection of single metal ions by stripping process. The stripping process involves the transfer/separation of metal ions from the targeted liquid and then the sensing analysis were performed with the help of electrolytes. To sense metal ions, in-situ voltammetry measurements were performed using prepared working electrodes. In particularly the sensing response of the as prepared samples was examined in comparison to Cd and Cr using electrochemical analysis. The MWCNT assisted Chitosan@MnO2 hybrid nanomaterial exhibited excellent sensitivity, perfect variability, and strong capability to detection of several heavy metal ions such as Chromium and cadmium ions.The Department of science and technology (DST -Delhi), India as sponsor this work under the sanction project ref. no. DST/TMD(EWO)/OWUIS-2018/TS-06.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) MnO2, (b) MWCNT, (c) Chitosan (d) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1, and (e) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2.
FTIR spectra of (a) MnO2, (b) MWCNT, (c) Chitosan (d) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1, and (3) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2.
FESEM images of (a) MnO2, (b) MWCNT, (c) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1, and (d) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2.
Elemental mapping images of (a) MnO2, (b) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1, and (c) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2.
EDAX spectra of (a) MnO2, (b) MWCNT (c) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1, and (d) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2.
(a–e) HR-TEM images of and (f) SAED pattern of MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1.
EIS spectra of (a) MnO2, (b) MWCNT, (c) Chitosan (d) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1, and (3) MWCNT/CQDs/MnO2-2.

Cyclic voltammograms of (A) 0.1 M of KCl solution and (B) 0.005 M of K3 [Fe(CN)6] at a scan rate of 50 mv/s in (a) pure graphite electrode, (b) Chitosan, (c) MWCNT, (d) MnO2, (e) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 and (f) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2.

DPV of modified graphite electrodes in 0.01 M acetate buffer solution (pH 5) containing 0.1 mg/L of Cd ion (A) preconcentration/stripping process and (B) direct contact method (a) pure graphite electrode, (b) chitosan, (c) MWCNT, (d) MnO2, (e) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 and (f) MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2.

MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2 electrodes (a) Sensing response for 10 ppm of chromium in acetate buffer solution (pH 6) and (b) Effect of pH in 0.1 M acetate buffer containing 0.1 mg/L of chromium.
(a) DPV response of MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 in the presence of Cr (VI) with changing concentration in 0.1 M acetate buffer solution at pH-6 and (b) linear calibration curve of Cr (VI) response.
(a) linear pot for standard deviation of three set of sensing measurements and (b) Sensing response of the as prepared electrodes in 0.1 M of electrolytes against Cr (VI) ions.
Schematic illustration for preparation of metal ion sensor and its sensing response.
Schematic illustration for mechanism of chromium ion absorption and sensing.
In-situ Sensing responses of the CQDs/MWCNT/MnO2 modified electrodes against real sample.

head.ce:title: Development of in-situ electrochemical heavy metal ion sensor using integrated 1D/0D/1D hybrid by MWCNT and CQDs supported MnO2 nanomaterial

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials and MEMS Laboratory, Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering, Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, 603 110, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering
Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, 603 110, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, SSN College of Engineering, Chennai, 603 110, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre of Excelllence in Water Research (CEWAR), Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, 603 110, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre of Excelllence in Water Research (CEWAR)
Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre of Excelllence in Water Research (CEWAR), Sri Sivasubramaniya Nadar College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, 603 110, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An integrated 1D/0D/1D hybrid nanomaterial was prepared from MWCNT supported carbon quantum dots @ MnO2 nanomaterial for a sensitive and selective electrochemical heavy metal ion sensor by hydrothermal methods. The developed nanomaterials were characterized by various analytical methods such as FESEM, HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, EDX and elemental mapping study, and also its electrochemical properties of the prepared samples were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) analysis has been used to investigate the quantitative detection of heavy metal ions such as cadmium and chromium on modified electrodes under optimal conditions. The in-situ electrochemical sensitivity and selectivity of the samples were determined by varying various parameters, such as the concentration of heavy metal ions, different electrolytes and electrolyte pH. The observed DPV results show that prepared MWCNT (0.05 wt%) and CQD (0.1 wt%) supported MnO2 nanoparticles show effective detection response for chromium (IV) metal ion. In particular, 0D CQD, 1D MWCNT, and MnO2 hybrid nanostructures produced a synergistic effect among them, resulting in strong electrochemical performance of the prepared samples against the target metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pollution by heavy metals of the soil, atmosphere and especially the water environment has become a serious problem with the development of urbanization. In general, a material can be described as heavy by its density, while it can be described as metal by its purity or by an alloy of metallic elements (Liu et al., 2019; Chaturvedi et al., 2018). Nutritionally, minerals like copper, iron, manganese, and zinc are essential for healthy living (Winiarska-Mieczan et al., 2019). Due to their nonbiodegradability, heavy metals remain in the environment and food chain forever, posing a serious threat to top-level predators (Ramdzan et al., 2020). Additionally, they are toxic and may cause serious diseases in the human body, for example, neurogenic disease, cancer, and organ failure, so it is important to detect them as soon as possible. To detect heavy metal ions, a variety of techniques have been developed, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS), electrochemical, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (R–FS), and mass spectroscopy (MS). In mass spectrometric and optical methods, various ions can be detected simultaneously with high selectivity, sensitivity and accuracy, but the process requires specialized personnel and relatively expensive instruments (Thiruppathi et al., 2017). Therefore, compared with other methods, electrochemical detection methods are of great interest due to the possibility of short evaluation time, low power consumption, high sensitivity, and easy adaptation to on-site measurements (Liu et al., 2017; Theerthagiri et al., 2020). The synthesized nanoparticles can be used to make sensitive contact and sensitive electrodes by adapting their functions and attaching them to the electrode surface, which leads to the selective detection of heavy metal ions (Ramu et al., 2020; Morillo Martín et al., 2018). To identify toxic metal ions, a variety of nanomaterials have been used, including carbon-based nanomaterials, silicon, polymeric nanomaterials, and metal nanoparticles (Guo et al., 2021; Theerthagiri et al., 2017).

In electrochemical sensors, functionalized nanoparticles (NPs) with a high metal ion attraction have been widely used to increase sensitivity, conductivity, and surface area (Aragay et al., 2011), (Sohrabi et al., 2021). The MnO2 nanostructures are extensively used for the detection of heavy metals owing to it several excellent characteristics including their low cost, non-toxic nature, high adsorption capacities nano-morphological properties, functional biocompatibility, and catalytic abilities (Li et al., 2020). On the other hand, in the electrochemical sensing of heavy metal ions, single wall and multiwall carbon nanotubes tend to be most commonly used (Moyo et al., 2014; Kokab et al., 2019). The 1D carbon nanotube is an intermediate form between fullerenes and graphite (Zaporotskova et al., 2016). A wide range of studies have established the effectiveness of multiwall carbon nanotube (MWCNT) for improving detection of heavy metal ion detection (Cui et al., 2015; Malakootian et al., 2020). Also, the MWCNT is suitable material for the production of electrochemical sensors due to its high sensitivity which adsorb the targeted molecules on its surfaces (Naik et al., 2021). Similarly, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have gained increasing attention because of its characteristics, including their ability to dissolve in water, their tunable fluorescence, their biocompatibility, and their chemical inertness, in addition to their ease and affordability of synthesis (Pawar et al., 2019). In general, CQD are smaller than 10 nm and contains carbon molecules with an amorphous to nanocrystalline core with sp2 carbons. In comparison to other carbon-based nanostructures, carbon QDs have a larger surface area which facilitates electrochemical reactions.

In this work, 1D/0D/1D hybrid nanomaterials were prepared by using the MWCNT and CQD supported MnO2 to sense the heavy metal ion through electrochemical analysis. The 1D/0D/1D hybrid nanomaterial containing CQD distributed on 1D MWCNT and MnO2 hybrid nanoparticle was formed by in-situ hydrothermal process. The structural, morphological and elemental analysis of the prepared samples were investigated through various analytical methods. In addition, the electrochemical properties of the as-prepared samples were examined using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Most of the recent research on electrochemical sensors has focused on the detection of single metal ions by stripping process. The stripping process involves the transfer/separation of metal ions from the targeted liquid and then the sensing analysis were performed with the help of electrolytes. To sense metal ions, in-situ voltammetry measurements were performed using prepared working electrodes. In particularly the sensing response of the as prepared samples was examined in comparison to Cd and Cr using electrochemical analysis. The MWCNT assisted Chitosan@MnO2 hybrid nanomaterial exhibited excellent sensitivity, perfect variability, and strong capability to detection of several heavy metal ions such as Chromium and cadmium ions.

The chemicals were all used directly from reagent-grade containers without any further purification. Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), Chitosan, Nitric acid (HNO3), MWCNT (Purity: 95%, diameters 20–40 nm and lengths 5–15 nm), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL) chemicals.

MnO2 nanoparticles were synthesized through a hydrothermal process by adding two mol of KMnO4 and 2 ml of 60% concentrated HNO3. Afterward, the mixture was moved to autoclave and kept at 150° for 8 h. Then the obtained product was washed repeatedly with double distilled water and ethanol, followed by overnight drying at 80°.

Carboxyl and hydroxyl groups are induced by acid treatment of the MWCNT surface. In brief, 0.05 g of MWCNT were dispersed into a 10 N solution of HNO3 and H2SO4 (2:8), which was kept under reflux for 12 h at 120 °C. The MWCNTs were then centrifuged out of the solution and thoroughly washed with deionized water up to the pH of residues solution reaches 7. Ater that, the obtained MWCNT were dried at 80 °C for overnight.

The MWCNT and CQD assisted MnO2 hybrid nanomaterials were prepared by a hydrothermal process similar that those used to prepare pristine MnO2 nanoparticles. As part of the synthesis process, different weight ratios of MWCNT and chitosan (0.05:0.1 (Named as MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1) and 0.1:0.05 (Named as MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2) were added with MnO2 precursor solution. Then, the reaction solution was transferred to a stainless-steel autoclave lined with Teflon and kept at 150 °C. for 8 h. After centrifugation and drying, the final product was obtained.

During the surface treatment of graphite electrodes, paraffin wax was mostly used to modify the unused surface of the graphite electrodes due to its acting as non-electrolytic binders also it is nonvolatile, water-immiscible, and chemically inert. Hence the purchased graphite electrodes were wax shielded with the help of a paraffine wax in vacuum chamber. The graphite electrodes were then modified as a working electrode by pouring 10 μl of freshly prepared samples (0.01 g/l).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) investigation was used to examine the crystal structure, diffraction patterns, and phase purity by using the Riguku miniflux-II X-ray diffractometer in with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54046 Å) in 2θ range between 10 and 80°. The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected by a Bruker Tensor-27 spectrophotometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The morphology, elemental mapping, and composition were investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), with elemental mapping and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) by JEOL Model JSM 6390LV, Jeol/JEM 2100, with the LaB6 source, and JEOL Model JED 2300 respectively. Electrochemical behaviors and sensing response of the prepared samples were analyzed by Admiral Squidstat Plus potentiostat/galvanostat/FRA.

The crystallinity, crystallite size, lattice spacing, and lattice planes were examined using XRD at over 2θ ranges between 10 and 80°. Fig. 1 shows XRD patterns of MnO2, MWCNT, chitosan, and different weight percentages of MWCNT and CQD assisted MnO2 nanoparticles. In the case of MnO2, the diffraction peaks were originated at 2θ values of 28.82, 37.33, 42.84, 56.72, 59.35, and 72.3° which revealed the crystal lattice at (011), (101), (111), (211), (220) and (112) respectively which are well-matched with the β phase of MnO2 (JCPDS File no. 24–0735) (Su et al., 2013) (Wang et al., 2013). In the hydrothermal reaction, no additional impurity phases are noticed, signifying that the as prepared sample have high purity phase of β-MnO2. Fig. 1(b) depicts the strong diffraction pattern found at 24.36° corresponds to the graphitic layered structure of MWCNT (Irfan et al., 2015). Fig. 1 (c) displays the XRD pattern of pure chitosan powder and the diffraction peaks observed at 15.1° and 20° exhibits the semicrystalline nature of chitosan-chromone derivative (Kumar and Koh, 2012).

In Fig. 1 (d) and (c) the XRD pattern of different weight percentages of MWCNT and CQD assisted MnO2 nanoparticles shows. The diffraction intensity was also reduced with the introduction of MWCNT and CQD with MnO2 nanoparticles which confirms the formation of the hybrid sample. The crystallite size of the samples was calculated by using the Deby Scherrer equation and the calculated average crystallite size of the MnO2, MWCNT, Chitosan, MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1, and MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2 nanohybrid samples are 76.5, 12.4, 6.82, 67.81, and 70.09 respectively. Similarly, the lattice strain values observed values are 0.0019, 0.0126, 0.0298, 0.0022, and 0.0021 for the MnO2, MWCNT, Chitosan, MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1, and MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2 nano hybrid samples respectively.

In Fig. 2 (b) the pristine MWCNT exhibits major characteristic peaks were observed at 3431 cm−1 and 1092 cm−1 were caused by the O–H stretching vibrations of adsorbed water and aromatic C–O, respectively (Kavinkumar and Manivannan, 2016). Similarly, the peaks observed at 1662 and 1590 cm−1 are ascribed to the CO and C–C stretching vibrations of the carboxylic groups (–COOH), respectively (Zaman et al., 2017). The existence carboxylic group confirm the presence of functionalized MWCNT which is obtained through acid treatment. MWCNT@CQD assisted MnO2 hybrid samples exhibit absorption peaks at 3674, 1714, 1538 and 400-600 cm−1 originating from the O–H, CO, C–O and M-O functional groups respectively which confirm the presence of MWCNT, Chitosan and MnO2 confirms formation of hybrid.

Fig. 3 show the FE-SEM images of as-prepared pure MnO2, MWCNT and different weight percentage of MWCNT and CQD with MnO2. The pristine MnO2 nanoparticle shows a rod-like structure and these rods are between 300 and 1000 nm in length and 20–50 nm in thickness. According to the FESEM micrographs (Fig. 3(c) and d), the addition of MWCNT and chitosan slightly changed the morphology of the MnO2 rods into a plate-like structure with heavy agglomeration. The strong electrostatic interaction between the CQD and MWCNT causes heavy agglomeration of the prepared nanoparticles.

An SEM with EDX mapping technique was applied to examine element distributions of the prepared samples. Fig. 4 (a) depicts an even homogenous distribution of Mn and O. Manganese, oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon are highly distributed in the MWCNT and CQD assisted MnO2 samples, as shown in Fig. 4 (b and c). The observed mapping analysis result shows the distribution of MWCNT, CQD, and MnO2 in the hybrid sample. Fig. 5 shows the EDX study of the prepared samples which confirms that the existence of the carbon, Mn and O, elements support the formation of hybrid samples with desired atomic and weight percentages.

The HR-TEM images and SAED patterns of the hybrid MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2 -1 nanomaterial is shown in Fig. 6 . Morphology analysis of the prepared samples indicated that MWCNT and CQD assisted MnO2 hybrid nanomaterials had been successfully formed. Fig. 6 (a and b) illustrates the dispersion of MnO2, and CQD on MWCNTs. In MWCNT/CQD/MnO2 hybrid nanoparticles, zero-dimensional CQD were well distributed on the surface of 1D MWCNT and MnO2 nanorods. The CQDs are almost uniform in size and highly mono-dispersed with an average size diameter of 2.181 ± 0.389 nm based on fitting the histogram to a Gaussian model.

In Fig. 6((c) (insert figure)), the particle size distribution histograms of the CQD diameters were determined by analyzing TEM images in Fig. 6 (d) situated at different locations. Similarly, the lattice spacing of MnO2, and CDS was found to be 0.24 and 0.21 nm which were identified to the (101) and (100) planes of MnO2, and CQD respectively which reveals the crystalline nature of the prepared samples. There is also evidence that some CQD are bonded to MnO2 nanoparticles, as well as some of CQD loaded MnO2 nanoparticles are glued to MNO2. Fig. 6 (f) shows a SAED pattern of the prepared sample exhibiting a ring-like diffraction pattern coupled with some additional bright spots. The MWCNTs, and CQDs can more contribute to the polycrystalline nature of the hybrid nanomaterial.

Using the EIS technique, the electrochemical behaviors of the prepared samples were studied. The impedance technique is an effective technique to explore the characteristics of a surface-modified working electrode. In this study, the electrochemical functions of modified electrodes with single or mixed components have been investigated. To investigate the conductivity and electron transfer properties of prepared samples, EIS analysis of modified electrodes was measured in 0.1 mol of KCl solution. Complex impedance is the sum of real Z (ω), and imaginary Z (ω) constituents, which are derived from the resistance and capacitance of the cell. A Nyquist plot (Z″vs. Z′) of the Faradaic impedance spectra for the prepared samples is shown in Fig. 7 . In Fig. 7. MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2 samples exhibit semicircle followed by a straight line in the low-frequency region, revealing a preferably good capacitive behavior of the prepared samples. Insert figure in 7(a) shows the equivalent circuit of the observed impedance spectra of the MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 hybrid sample. The charge transfer resistance of MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 hybrid was much lower than that of pure MnO2 electrodes, implying that the MWCNT increased the conductivity ofhybrid sample. The MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 electrode sample has excellent electrochemical performance, implying improved electron transfer on the surfaces of the modified electrodes.

The electrochemical assets of the synthesized samples were examined using cyclic voltammetry (CV) in a solution which contains 0.1 M of KCl and 0.005 M of a K3[Fe (CN)6] redox probe in a solution containing 0.1 M of KCl. Fig. 8 exhibit cyclic voltammograms of bare graphite electrodes and modified electrodes. In CV, a pair of reversible redox peaks were detected, which were attributed to one-electron electrochemical reaction of Fe (CN)63−/4−. As a consequence of the conductive properties of MWCNT, the MWCNT and CQD assisted MnO2 hybrid samples show improved redox peak currents. However, the peak current decreased slightly in the 0.1:0.5 wt% of MWCNT: CQD assisted MnO2 hybrid sample which may be due to the effect of high concentration of CQD which may block the electron transfer between electrolyte and electrode surface. In addition, the observations demonstrate that the as prepared samples were successfully immobilized on the graphite electrodes.

In general, the deposition, standing, stripping, and cleaning process are the major steps for the electrochemical measurement of metal ions. The target analyte is typically preconcentrated on the electrode surface used for anodic stripping analysis, using a modification electrode material. In general, two electrochemical reactions occurred on the surface of the WE during DPV analysis's sensing assessment of metal ions. Initially, the electrode surface is exposed to a negative electrodeposition potential for a fixed period to deposit heavy metals; second, these deposited metal atoms are oxidized to metal ions under adequate DPV conditions. During the deposition process on the working electrode, a deposition potential of −1.2 V has been used for 60 s. After standing for 10s, the stripping process started with DPV potential scanning of −1 to 0 V. Deposition and cleaning were among the processes that required stirring. Initially the Cd ions was reduced in a 0.1 M acetate buffer for some period of time in order to deposit Cd (IV) on modified electrodes. Then the DPV responses of the electrodes were recorded from −1.0 to 0 V at the pure graphite electrode, chitosan, MWCNT, MnO2, MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2 nanohybrid modified electrodes in fresh acetate buffer (0.1 M and pH 4.5) containing 0.1 mg/L of Cd (II). There is no considerable electrochemical response were observed for pristine MnO2 and MWCNT samples modified electrodes through the preconcentration process. The pristine chitosan modified electrode shows a response corresponding to the oxidation of Cd ions potential captured Cd (II) at −0.58 V. It is assumed that the Cd II ions were attracted by the amino and hydroxyl group of chitosan, then reduced the M(II) in to M (0) and accumulated on the electrode surfaces at a specific potential.

Also, the sensing responses of the modified electrodes were analyzed by in-situ sensing process. During this process, the sensing performance of modified graphite electrodes was examined directly in a buffer solution of 0.1 M acetate buffer containing 0.01 mM/L of Cd(II) ions. However, sensing the cadmium ions using modified graphite electrodes MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2 shows some promising results (Fig. 9 ). It was believed that the hydroxyl and carboxylic groups serve as anchoring sites to adsorbs the heavy metals on the MWCNT surface which enhances the sensitivity of the prepared samples. Similarly, the remarkable DPV response of the modified MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 electrode can be reasonably attributed to the affinity of the MWCNT and CQD particles towards the metal ion adsorption. The above experimental results demonstrate that the MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 hybrid nanocomposite modified electrode is used to detection of metal ions due to it has excellent electrochemical sensitivity under the direct contact method.

Using the same method and analysis, the sensing ability of the prepared samples was determined against the chromium ions and the observed results were shown in Fig. 10 (b). The DPV signal at NHs/GE electrode for 0.1 μg/L chromium in 0.1 M acetate buffer. This peak is associated with the stripping of reduced chromium species during stripping ranges from −0.2 V to 1.2 V. Increasing in response current was recorded with the additions of cadmium and the resultant plot of MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2 were given in Fig. 10 (a) and (b). At 0.7 V, a clear and well-defined peak of Cr (VI) was obtained using a potential range of 0 V–1 V. The impact of accumulation potential on the sensing of Cr (VI) was examined using a 0.1 M acetate buffer at pH 6 containing 5 gL-1 of Cr (VI), and the results are revealed in Fig. 10. Peak currents of the Cr (VI) were greatest when the accumulation potential was −0.5V, as shown in Fig. 10 (a). As part of evaluating the sensitivity of the prepared samples, DPV response to different Cr (VI) concentrations under optimized pH conditions was measured from pH 4 to 9. When chromium (VI) is detected at different pH levels the MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 shows a high sensing response in all pH ranges (Fig. 10(b)). The observed efficient sensing response of the MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 particularly owed to the high electrical conductivity, electrocatalytic activity, and a high surface-to-volume ratio of MWCNT, which made them ideal electron intermediaries in electrochemical detection of cadmium (VI). Also, the CQD supports the adsorption of Cr (VI) on the surface of the working electrode. Likewise, the synergistic effect of MnO2 and bi-conjugated MWCNT with CQD were improves the sensitivity of the Cr (VI) metal ion detection. Particularly At pH-6, the MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 exhibits improved sensitivity to the chromium ions. Using the sensing results obtained, different concentrations of cadmium (IV) ions sensing experiments were carried out with the help of acetated buffer solution (pH 6).

Fig. 11 (a) illustrates that the concentration of Cr (VI) in the electrolyte solution increases (from 10 to 100 L), the current signal of a prepared sample is gradually increased. The resulting linear calibration plot is linear over the range from 10 t0 100 μg/L. A sensor's sensitivity and limit of detection are the two most important parameters to evaluate its performance when it comes to assessing analytical processes. The limit of detection of the prepared sample was obtained by the linear plot. Based on the linear plot the correlation coefficients LOD and LOQ values were calculated which are 0.9999, 0.99, μM and 0.302 μM respectively. Likewise, the linear performance of the calibration plot indicates that diffusion control prevails within the studied concentration range. Also the.

In Fig. 12 (a) shows the linear fit for the standard deviation sensing response of MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 against Cr ion. The standard deviation associated with the sensing response is not significant which is good for these real time measurements. Also the insignificant deviation suggesting the strong stability of the prepared samples.

Similarly, the nature of the electrolytes can impact the complex formation of metal ions on the working electrodes active surface. Studies with various background electrolytes were performed at the 0.1 M concentrations and its observed results were shown in Fig. 12(b). The sensing response of the prepared samples were scanned at various electrolyte medium such as acetate buffer (ABS) phosphate buffer (PBS) and sodium sulfate and potassium sulfate. In all electrolyte medium the MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 shows effective sensing response against chromium ion. Particularly, 0.1 M of acetate buffer has the maximum response for MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 as well as MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-2 samples. As a result, 0.1 M acetate buffer solution was chosen as the background electrolyte for further study.

Fig. 13 shows the diagrammatic representation of the preparation of metal ion sensor. A CQD's adsorptive characteristic can attract metal ions towards the WE electrode surfaces, while an MWCNT acts as a charge-transfer medium with excellent conductivity. In this work, the as prepared pristine and hybrid samples show low sensing response in the presence of Cr (VI) as well as Cd (II) metal ions. However, the main motivation of this work is fabrication and characterization of direct contact sensors with the presence of metal ions.

Fig. 14 shows the diagrammatic representation of the sensing mechanism. Due to the synergetic effects of MWCNT, CQD and MnO2 nanoparticles, the MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 sample works according to in-situ sensing process, and thus shows improved sensing ability against the Cr (VI) metal ion. Also, the observed results show that the approximate amount of dimensionally mixed hybrid (1D/0D/1D) nanomaterial is the best among individual nanoparticles due to the low agglomeration and synergistic effect as well as improved electrical conductivity of the prepared samples.

Finally, the sensing response of the as developed metal ion sensor were tested against the real wastewater and its experimental result was shown in Fig. 15 . The observed result clearly shows the sensing response of the prepared samples and its wide sensing response shows the presence various contamination in the polluted water.

The hydrothermal method was successfully applied to produce dimensionally (1D/0D/1D) heterogenous MWCNT/CQDs/MnO2 hybrid nanoparticles. Structural, morphological, and electrochemical properties of the obtained samples were systematically analyzed by a wide range of analytical methods. DPV analysis was used to examine the metal ion detection function of Cd2+ and Cr6+ ions. The MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 hybrid show excellent sensing properties against Cr6+ with LOD of 0.999 and 0.32 μg/L. The CQD and MWCNT improve the metal ion absorptivity and conductivity of the hybrid sample and therefore increase the sensor conductivity. At pH 6 in an acetate buffer solution, the new sensor shows higher sensitivity than other pH and electrolyte solutions. This may be due to the strong electrochemical reaction between the analyte and the sensor. In addition, CQD, MWCNT, and MnO2 nanoparticles synergistically increase the metal ion detection efficiency, which in turn increases the electron transfer rate at the 1D/0D/1D nanostructures in the MWCNT/CQDS/MnO2-1 sample. In addition, the multi-dimensional hybrid nanostructure and sensing mechanism of the proposed sensor can be suitable for applications such as detecting trace metals in environmental samples in the field or in industrial samples.

K. Bhuvaneswari: Conceptualization; Investigation; Writing -Original Draft; Visualization, S. Radha: Supervision, Data curation: Project administration; B S Sreeja: Methodology, Writing - Review & Editing. P. Senthil Kumar: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Department of science and technology (DST -Delhi), India as sponsor this work under the sanction project ref. no. DST/TMD(EWO)/OWUIS-2018/TS-06 .
